original_abstract:
Air pollution represents a critical global health crisis, contributing to millions of premature deaths annually. To mitigate these risks, public authorities require robust forecasting tools to implement preemptive measures against high pollutant concentrations. Machine Learning (ML), and specifically Deep Learning (DL), has emerged as a dominant methodology for modeling complex atmospheric data. This paper provides an extensive systematic survey of the progress in air quality forecasting between 2011 and 2021. Following a rigorous selection process across major scientific databases, 155 representative studies were analyzed and categorized.

The review examines the literature through several lenses: geographical focus, target pollutants, input variables, evaluation metrics, and specific algorithmic architectures. Our analysis reveals a significant correlation between national pollution levels and research output, with a steady increase in publications until a shift toward pandemic-related time-series research in 2021. We find that the Air Quality Index (AQI) and PM2.5 are the most frequently predicted metrics, and that integrating meteorological data with historical pollutant levels consistently yields the highest predictive accuracy. Furthermore, we observe that while traditional regression models like Random Forest and SVR remain relevant, Deep Learning architectures—particularly LSTMs and hybrid models—are increasingly favored for their superior ability to capture non-linear spatio-temporal dependencies. This survey concludes by highlighting emerging trends such as Graph Neural Networks and Transformers, providing a roadmap for future research in environmental informatics.
humanized_abstract:
Air pollution is a serious global health challenge that causes an estimated 3 million premature deaths per year. It is essential for authorities to have accurate air quality forecast to be able to take preventive countermeasures against harmful episodes. Over the years, Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) have been at the forefront in modelling complex atmospheric phenomena. In this study, a thorough systematic literature review on Air Quality Forecasting using ML/DL techniques from the years 2011-2021 is presented. A total of 155 impactful studies were identified after an extensive literature search of leading journals and databases, and were subsequently grouped accordingly.

This review paper organizes the existing literature on modeling time-series air quality data from various perspectives such as geographical locations, pollutants of interest, input variables used, evaluation metrics and finally, the machine learning algorithms employed. Our summary reveals a positive relationship between emissions of a country and the air quality modeling literature, which is increasing each year except in 2021 when the number of time-series studies on SARS-CoV-2 transmission shifted towards COVID-19. The Air Quality Index (AQI) and PM2.5 were the most popular targets. The highest prediction performances were achieved when historical measured concentrations of air pollutants at particular locations are combined with atmospheric variables. While simple regression-based models such as Random Forest and Support Vector Regression are still commonly used in the air quality literature, long short-term memory (LSTM) based Deep Learning models as well as their hybrids have recently become a popular choice for air quality modeling due to their capability of revealing complex temporal and spatial correlations inherent in the air quality data. Finally, we list Graph Neural Networks and Transformers which are emerging new paradigms in environmental informatics.
